These changes revert everything done to make gmpy2 a python package. This breaks tools and use cases which require a python package, binary wheels with dynamic linking most important one.

Furthermore, Installing library headers as `data_files` is problematic. It means they will be installed under `site-packages/src/..` and will not be found under the package's install directory. So cython C level imports (`#include <..>/gmpy2.h`) will not be able to find the headers under the gmpy2 package directory.

On a lesser note, it is considered bad practice to install package files like headers as data files. The `gmpy2_install_data` stuff does not look correct for binary wheel installations either and I'm not sure setting install dir as if the package was pure python is correct either.

If the cython cimports are not working, let's fix that. The existing `__init__.pxd` was attempting to do that. 

`test_cython/runtests.py` fails with the old setup.py as well (`AttributeError: 'module' object has no attribute '_C_API'`) for me, so what is the output supposed to be from that script?

The issues seems to be that the `from gmpy cimport *` line in `__init__.pxd` is not being picked up, so the extension is only available under `gmpy2.gmpy2`. That is an easy fix:

`from gmpy2.gmpy2 cimport *` in `__init__.pxd`

Now works the same as before, as in I get same error as `setup_legacy.py` -- 

```
from gmpy2 cimport *
import_gmpy2()
```

```AttributeError: 'module' object has no attribute '_C_API'```

I will raise a PR for the above change.

It would also be good to include `test_cython/runtests.py` in the automated tests. All the automated testing passed with the #178 changes, so this regression was never picked up.

I've continued to think about this issue for awhile.  Curio has grown some new functions for launching processes, performing work, and so forth.  Some consistency is order.    I'm going to modifiy curio to use a common signature for "launching" work whether it be a task, a thread, a process, or whatever:

    async def coro(x, y, z):
            ...

    await some_kind_of_work(coro, x, y, z, options=value)

Keyword arguments, if any, are reserved for options related to `some_kind_of_work` function, not the coro/func being called.   If keyword arguments need to be passed to the target, `partial()` can be used. 

Because of some backwards compatibility concerns with existing code, passing an instantiated coroutine to certain functions (spawn, run, etc.) will still work.    However, documentation and examples will prefer the new approach.

로직부분에 대한 리뷰가 정말 필요한데, 문제원인과 해결법을 고민해주셔서 감사합니다. 

  1 - 2.  

저도 어절의 오른쪽 (R part) 가 제대로 잡히지 않은 경우를 어떻게 처리해야하나 고민하다가 postprocessing 을 생각했었습니다. 

만약 명사 = {"카메", "카메라"} / 조사 = {"라는", '는"} 이 있다면 '카메라는 = 카메라 + 는, 카메 + 라는'으로 분해될 수 있을텐데, longest R 만 이용한다면, '카메라'가 잡히지 않을거라 생각했었습니다. 

그런데 '카메라'가 정말로 명사라면 어자피 '라는'의 경우가 아니더라도 다른 조사들 덕분에 명사로 추출될 가능성이 높겠네요. 

수정해주신 코드 부분을 보니, 수정한대로 계산하는 것이 더 안전하겠다는 생각이 듭니다.  **보내주신 코드를 곧바로 merge** 하도록 하겠습니다. 


  3 - 4.

해당 명사 점수 계산 함수는 단일 substring 에 대한 명사 점수를 계산합니다. "안녕", "안녕하", "안녕하십", "안녕하십니" 에 대해서 독립적으로 점수를 계산합니다. 

3, 4 에서 이야기해주시는 문제는 어쩌면 어절에서 명사를 잘라내는 새로운 함수로 해결해야 하지 않나라는 생각이 듭니다.

명사 점수와 어절의 구조 (명사 오른쪽에 등장하는 글자)를 살펴서 어절에서 명사를 잘라내는 방향으로 함수를 개발해보도록 하겠습니다.

Think this situation: because of network delay, or because of large responses by bulk execution, the response of the pervious command may arrive after cleaning the buffer. def main(num):

@argh.arg('num', nargs='?')

Try using the full 16 byte value - the 4 byte notation is BLE shorthand for:

```
0000XXXX-0000-1000-8000-00805F9B34FB
```

where `XXXX` is replaced by those 4 bytes. In your case, try `00003EA0-0000-1000-8000-00805F9B34FB` for the first characteristic you mentioned.

Original comment by: Dmitry Shachnev

@devcurmudgeon That spec seems to be suggesting an extension to the "put the license in every file" technique, which adds to the duplicity and potential for deviation. What happens if one declares one license in the SPDX but uses the text of another (or tweaks the text)? What does it mean of some files have different licenses than others? What does it mean if a file is contributed to the project without a license or with a conflicting license? From my brief skim, it doesn't solve the problem of referencing a license in metadata at the project level and applying that license to files in the project.

What I'd really like is a way to _declare_ a license without having to spell it out in each project (or worse in each file). The declaration should cascade, such that a single reference at the project level will apply to all files in the project, but still allow projects to override or extend the license at the project or file or component level, much in the way cascading style sheets work for HTML. It should appear declared in the source and that declaration should be incorporated into the package build, such that a developer could simply in one place declare the license for the project and let the tooling handle the rest.

Ok... so the way to solve your problem is to change the `selectcolor` to black.

The selectcolor is the colour of the square where to tick appears

![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/2329501/38987461-c5a7cd98-43c7-11e8-8593-621ef5171c17.png)

```python
import tkinter
from guizero import App, CheckBox

app = App(title="test", layout="grid",bg="#000000")
LeftRight_Link_Checkbox = CheckBox(app,"Link\nArrays",grid=[5,0])
LeftRight_Link_Checkbox.bg="#000000"
LeftRight_Link_Checkbox.text_color="#ffffff"
LeftRight_Link_Checkbox.tk.config(selectcolor="#000000")

app.display()
```

If you wanted to stop it flashing back when you click it you also need to change "activebackground" and "activeforeground".

check.tk.config(activebackground="#000000")
check.tk.config(activeforeground="#ffffff")

As a fix for guizero, CheckBox should set "selectcolor" and "activebackground" to the `bg` color and "activeforeground" to the `text_color`.

For a rough get you started, the main function that is expected to be used
is the following:

https://github.com/CCA-Physics/pymedphys/blob/master/pymedphys/level1/mudensity.py#L348

mu, mlc, and jaw are all numpy arrays. mu is a one dimensional array, mlc a
three dimensional array, jaw is two dimensional.

The first dimension is always control point number. The last dimension is
mlc/jaw bank number. For mlc's the middle dimension is leaf pair number.

The coordinate system is taken to be bipolar to match the coordinate system
used by the Elekta linac service mode.

Distances are to be all in mm.

Hopefully that covers the basics.

I've spent the morning digging into this and here's what little I did come up with.

`__path__` is available in Python 2 as seen here: https://docs.python.org/2/tutorial/modules.html#packages-in-multiple-directories

From what I can tell though extensions packages are not given a __path__. I may be wrong with this but I can't find anything connecting the two.

I dug into `import.c` and focused on `find_module`. From what I can tell, `__path__` is not used at all and all possible attempts at providing a path resulted in "No frozen submodule named ..." which is the same error you get if you try to import a submodule for a module path that hasn't been imported yet.

Python 2.7.10:
`import.c` http://pastebin.com/kRUjSVyT
`importdl.c` http://pastebin.com/qKUjSdq8

I think the more interesting bit is that siege.pyd is considered a `imp.C_EXTENSION` which, when using `imp.load_module()` will end up calling `_PyImport_LoadDynamicModule`. This has no mention of `__path__` only `load_package()` does which is called from `load_module()` but that's only called for modules of type `imp.PKG_DIRECTORY`.

This is the most I could gleam from it for now. I've never dug into Python source before so this is all very unfamiliar territory to me. I'm considering posting in stackoverflow.com about this but wanted to first post here.
No worries, I'm away for most of the weekend myself. Let me know when it's done, and get well soon!

Since we don't execute anything in Python files, there's no reason to use something like `__completions__` anyway. This will also not change, because if you don't start executing Python code you are going down a very dangerous path.

Compiled files (builtins, etc) are executed at the moment, but this might even change once we understand typeshed.

I think the cleanest way would be to distinguish between python3 and python2 at this place.

    if sys.version_info.major >= 3:
        import importlib
        # ....
    else:
        import imp
        # ...


Actively ignoring deprecation warnings is imho not a good idea because things will probably break in the future.
Also: the API is just not where it will be. The API is quite weird and complicated and will be way easier.

Maybe we need some kind of a Context object which would contain things like that, but that would probably be too much of a refactoring, so I am ok with using reader/writer->connection->login for now, I think this is how other serializers do that currently anyway.

SimpleProducer is deprecated. I'd highly recommend using the KafkaConsumer / KafkaProducer interfaces if you want to use kafka-python 1.1.1 .

The error message here indicates that there is a corrupt internal connection receive buffer. This buffer holds bytes internally when reading from a socket if/when the full message has not been fully delivered. I have not seen this error get thrown in normal use, but that doesn't mean there's not a bug somewhere. As the message suggests, the most likely culprit is unsynchronized, concurrent access to the receive buffer.

Looking at your code, I might also suggest that you not reuse the same client instance across all producers/consumers. This is one of the many flaws of the Simple* interfaces -- they suggest that reusing a single client is helpful (it is usually not).

Hi @prtome!

The output widget does not differentiate between child and parent rows. So it will output rows as set by the options.

There is a [`output_hiddenColumns` option](http://mottie.github.io/tablesorter/docs/example-widget-output.html#output_hiddencolumns) (`false` by default) which will only include hidden columns if `true` and the column is hidden using `display: none`.

You can make the output widget ignore set columns using the [`output_ignoreColumns` option](http://mottie.github.io/tablesorter/docs/example-widget-output.html#output_ignorecolumns).

And if you want *all* rows to be output, set the [`output_saveRows` option](http://mottie.github.io/tablesorter/docs/example-widget-output.html#output_saverows) to `'a'` (or `'all'`).

If none of those help fix the issue, then please modify [this demo](http://jsfiddle.net/Mottie/abkNM/2617/) and describe the expected output.

The whole point of using Decimal datatype is to be exact, so I would consider this a by-design behaviour. To avoid the error, you can either give the exact value as strings to Decimal constructor,
```
for testval in (Decimal('8550123.2'), Decimal('85000.2')):
```
or use floating point datatype directly:
```
for testval in (8550123.2, 85000.2):
```

eso file. I think I might have taken a shortcut when parsing the file in
that the timestamps do not get parsed into the the resulting structure -
something that I would be glad to fix if this is needed, but that would
require using a datastructure like the pandas timeseries as opposed to just
plain python arrays.

We can also discuss changes to esoreader to make it easier to use for eppy

@jmswenski I refactored things a bit, so if you pull the master branch as of today, the API has changed slightly (for the better I hope).

Basically, the change is that the methods all take a "camera" or "basestation" instead of "basestation_id", "camera_id", "xcloud_id", etc. All of that data is actually part of the device schema, so the methods just get it for you.

That being said, the example I posted yesterday would change slightly to be like so:
```
arlo = Arlo(USERNAME, PASSWORD)

basestations = arlo.GetDevices('basestation')

def callback(arlo, basetstation, event):
    print("motion event detected!")
    # Here you will have access to self, basestation, and the event schema.

arlo.SubscribeToMotionEvents(basestation[0], callback)```

There's also a more generic `HandleEvents()` method which is called by SubscribeToMotionEvents(). This new method can be used to handle ALL events produced by the event stream if you want. You'd just have something like a big switch statement for each event type in the callback that you pass.

```def HandleEvents(self, basestation, callback, timeout=None)```

Yes, these characters can be entered into Excel by e.g. copy-pasting them from your browser into Excel.
U+FFFE: ￾
U+FFFF: ￿

Excel encodes these character identically to the way `xlsxwriter` does with the above mentioned fix, e.g. `_xFFFE_`.

Regardless of Excel's support for these Unicode non characters, it is a violation of `xlsxwriter`'s `write_string` API to generate an invalid Excel file if the string happens to contain U+FFFF or U+FFFE.

You can already do this.  The ansible-toolbox commands don't provide ansible with any inventory, so ansible just uses it's normal mechanism for locating an inventory.  You can use the `--hosts` (`-H`) option to specify a host other than localhost.

In other words, if you have in your `~/.ansible.cfg`:

    [default]
    inventory = ~/.ansible_hosts

And in `~/.ansible_hosts` you have:

    remote1 ansible_host=127.0.0.1

Then you can simply run:

    ansible-eval -H remote1 '{{ ansible_eth0 }}'

> I think the idea of doing a 2d transfer function indeed is a good idea. We
> can give it a shape of (N,M,4), where N is the f direction, and M the f'
> direction, and M can be of length 1 (basically a 1d transfer function).
>
> A practical problem is how to store it, f(\vec{x}) is stored in the alpha
> channel, and the derivative (normalized since it is used for lighting only
> now) in RGB. We could store it unnormalized, but since it is just 8 bits
> (-128,127) the resolution wil be quite poor. Another solution would be to
> store the spherical angles for the normal in say the R and G channel, and
> the amplitude in B.
>
> I'm not sure if i'm a fan of setting directly the rgba values, since that
> would make it impossible to change the transfer function interactively
> sincevolumetric datasets are often quite big, and transferring it can take
> a lot of time. Just updating the transfer function is much cheaper, and can
> happen on the JS side, meaning it does not need a running kernel.

Hi @maxtepkeev,

I totally agree that my server was misconfigured and that it did not respect the http protocol. It said _ok I'm sending you what you asked for_ but it didn't so you threw an exception because the content you received was not as expected. The name of the exception clearly indicates that: `JSONDecodeError`

I'm just saying that from the user point of view, when I got the exception without any details, I was like... _ok ... what now? Is it really malformed JSON or is it a bug in the parsing process?_ So the natural next step is to try and take a look at the content (what else could I do? Give up? :smile:)

Basically, by throwing this exception you're saying _I got bad json_ and the user will always want to know why you think it's bad or what you got, so that he can try and fix it. The internal exception would probably be helpful too because it'd probably be something like _Unexpected token <: expected {_ or something like that but really, I think the content is the key to understand this type of error.

On last example and I rest my case :wink: Imagine you get a `SocketError`. You'll start sniffing around the network or so ... But a `SocketError` saying _Connection could not be made because the remote party actively refused it_, you immediately know where to look: check the connection port, or whether the server's running or whether some firewall is in the way...

I guess my point is just that an exception should contain useful information to troubleshoot the error (besides its primary purpose of interrupting the control flow of the application). And so I think maybe in case of an error http status, like 401, 403 etc... you could also throw the response body along with the exception: it will probably contain something to clarify the error.

But again, thanks for the software, it's really helpful! :+1:

There is an issue with this PR as numpy.sum on generator return the same generator. Reason is that numpy expression are not Lazy in numpy as numpy need to know the size of the generator to allocate memory. For this reason, replacing list with generator will never work on numpy functions. Of course, we may still want to replace some numpy call on list to numpy call to generator to avoid memory allocation. 

I see 2 solution:
1) The filter-> ifilter transformation is perform after others (so after constant folding) and we accept to have non complient python at this stage. It could be done 
2) We may add an attributes to builtin like ReadOnlyNDArray and in this case, the filter can be transform to -> numpy.fromiter(ifilter(...)) but it supports only 1D array (which is most of the cases but not all) and we don't have the size information which may be required to create a numpy_expr.

Any better idea? Or should I just leave this PR as it can't be really applied?

Hmm... there are various sorts of clustering questions in regard to novelty and similar. For input space, how does one cluster together similar situations, for a variety of purposes? The simplest clustering methods tend to work off of distances - either single distances or distances that can be combined together using euclidean or other metrics (manhattan, maximum...). Looking at multiple distance measures at the same time is somewhat analogous to multiobjective optimization. There are other means - if temporality is present, then what input space portions tend to lead to the same next-time-around (dependent on the actions taken...) portion of the input space, for instance (admittedly dependent itself on definitions of "same"...)? Other ways of looking at input space can be "what variables tend to vary together" - can one simplify it by taking into account correlations (Principal Components Analysis for relatively-linear cases, for instance)? Yet another way to look at it is if one has a set of learners - NEAT, decision/regression trees, or whatever - are there patterns in how they are giving the same result or different results; areas of disagreement tend to be ones that need to be broken up further. The area of unsupervised learning also has some potentially-useful algorithms for finding patterns.

Once one has some idea on input clustering, then the problem tends to become how to find distances between behaviors when inputs are similar (and then clustering the close ones for analysis purposes, finding the best members of a Hall of Fame, or whatever). Of course, here too one can have questions about multiple distances (not only in different behavioral parameters - how much does one count differences in a particular output, or a particular hidden node? - but if behavior is very similar for part of the input space but very different for another part, for instance).

The above are my thoughts off the top of my head, and thus rather vague in spots... but hopefully may inspire some useful ideas.

I find this is actually a bug. The `pred` will be reshaped if it has ndim=1, see https://github.com/apache/incubator-mxnet/blob/master/python/mxnet/metric.py#L911-L912. In this case, the `pred` and the `label` have both shape (4,) and after reshaping, `pred.shape` will be changed to  (4,1). `pred - label` will then generate an array with shape (4, 4) and give the incorrect output.

1. My MXNet installation was done on Raspberry Pi 3 with Raspbian stretch and I followed the installation at https://mxnet.incubator.apache.org/get_started/install.html with "Devices" and "Raspberry Pi" selected.
2. For dependencies, I had to add installing the cblas package:

4. Install the python binding with sudo:
`cd python
sudo pip install --upgrade pip
sudo pip install -e .`

I'm incredibly sorry this isn't coming across correctly in text, as I'm trying to tie together a few concepts and as I noted above and the older versions of bleach are broken.  

Let me try and rephrase this differently.  The core concept is "The breaking change this PR introduces is the encoding of all HTML entities."  That breaks how input is visually displayed.  This problem also dovetails with some existing undocumented issues, which includes the URL (as I will explain in a moment)

Previous versions of bleach handled HTML entities incorrectly- they were decoded to unicode and then rendered as-is.  

    >>> print bleach.clean("•")
    •
    >>> print bleach.clean("•").__repr__()
    u'\u2022'

As this PR encodes the `&` , the new output is the following:

    >>> print bleach.clean("•")
    &bull;

This would render on a page as `&bull;` and not the bullet character `•`.    This is the breaking change.

The related issue with URLs breaking should probably be a new issue as it is broken with existing code too.  I'll create a new ticket for that discussion and cross-reference.

Following #293 it is now possible to set the config option `DATABASE_BACKEND` to select between different database technologies. Only "mongo" is currently supported, obviously. However, it means to add support for Postgres create a `postgres.py` file in the `database` package directory and implement the list of methods. Then set `DATABASE_BACKEND='postgres'` in your local config file to use Postgres instead of MongoDB.

The inputs are different. The input you're providing to the playground includes an `@context` which allows the processor to interpret the data. The input you're providing to pyld doesn't have any `@context` so the processor drops the "ical" property (it has no mapping) and it interprets the other properties as full URIs with the "ical" scheme (not CURIEs) and keeps them.

Instead, provide a context along with your input. You can do this either by injecting an `@context` property into the input, or you may use the `expandContext` option in the API:

```python
jsonld.compact(doc, context, {'expandContext': context});
```

Note that compaction is typically used to convert a document from one context to a different one, but here you're compacting to the same context. Depending on your use case, you may just be able to attach an `@context` property to the dictionary and be done. If you do really require some processing to be done, however, the above seems to be what you're looking for.

Here's what I ended up doing (and this information will be in the documentation with the 0.4.0 release as well):

 - Added two new keyword arguments to `PGPMessage.new`: `encoding` and `format`. Setting `encoding` will add a "Charset" header to the ASCII armor of the message (unless it is a cleartext message, in which case the Charset header will appear in the signature block).

 - If binary data is given when creating a PGPMessage, it will be left as-is unless `format` is explicitly set to `'u'` or `'t'`, or if `cleartext` is True.

 - if `cleartext` is True or `format` is set to `'u'` or `'t'` and `encoding` is set to something other than `utf-8`, PGPMessage will try to transcode the binary data given from the specified encoding to UTF-8.

Other than that, it should behave as it did before but without munging anything. Let me know if you find any cases where it still breaks :)

This was one of the lessons learned from boto.  If I remember correctly, there were issues with round tripping float values and that the built in float() type could not handle the 38 digits of precision supported in dynamodb's numeric types.  This would result in not being able to delete items in the table:

```
>>> d = Decimal('1234567890123.12345678901234567890')
>>> d
Decimal('1234567890123.12345678901234567890')
>>> float(d)
1234567890123.1235
```

More background info:

https://github.com/boto/boto/issues/873

PR:
https://github.com/boto/boto/pull/1183

Perhaps we could support using ints() if there's no floating point in the number.  Would need to investigate what the impact of that would be.

@jamesls The signer was rejecting the request because the header wasn't included in the request. When you actually do send the header, you get a different error: ```python import botocore sess = botocore.session.get_session() sns = sess.create_client('sts') url = sns.generate_presigned_url('get_caller_identity', {}) response = requests.post( url, headers={ 'content-type': 'application/x-www-form-urlencoded; charset=utf-8' } ) print(response.text) ``` ```xml Sender IncompleteSignature When Content-Type:application/x-www-form-urlencoded, URL cannot include query-string parameters (after '?'): '/?Action=GetCallerIdentity&Version=2011-06-15&X-Amz-Algorithm=AWS4-HMAC-SHA256&X-Amz-Credential=redacted%2F20171212%2Fus-east-1%2Fsts%2Faws4_request&X-Amz-Date=20171212T220209Z&X-Amz-Expires=3600&X-Amz-SignedHeaders=content-type%3Bhost&X-Amz-Signature=signature' 1a16614a-df88-11e7-af03-a144f0113aaf ``` While that error does come from the remote signer, it is fundamentally a serialization error.

I think we need to have a config option to enable/disable this.  Especially given that users can reject requests without unsigned payloads via IAM policies (http://docs.aws.amazon.com/AmazonS3/latest/API/bucket-policy-s3-sigv4-conditions.html), we need to give them a way to enable this behavior if needed.

I think I still prefer only doing this for the streaming s3 requests (put_object/upload_part) but I'm interested to hear other's thoughts.

For a little bit more information about what we're doing and why, Kinto is a database with a simple HTTP-based API that is designed to enable client-server synchronization. It's built to be extensible using plugins, with plugins being able to add endpoints or hook into various events (records being changed, for example). Some standard database features are implemented as plugins in Kinto, namely a "quotas" system preventing users from creating/updating records beyond a certain fixed storage limit. The quotas plugin is implemented by listening to a ResourceChanged event, and the listener to that event raises if the quota is exceeded. (The ResourceChanged event was fired in a beforeCommit hook, which was invoked by the pyramid_tm tween calling commit().)

> The idea here as I mentioned is that the environment is disposable, so we are not going through the overhead of trying to maintain its 'cleanliness' at this point.

For what it's worth, this is surprising to me.  I would not have thought pipenv considers environments disposable.  IMO, the environments created on a developers machine are not disposable.  pipenv reuses one if it's available every time there is a shell or run command.  A user would need to manually delete the current environment to get a new one.  In fact, I think the existence of the `run` command shows that pipenv is assuming environments will be long lived.  That is, you set it up with the packages you want and it will be available for later use.

> I can see arguments in favor of doing that

Hopefully to strengthen those arguments, here are a few situations where I think a "sync" command that did what this issue is asking for would be helpful:

* As a developer, when coming back to a project, to make sure my existing environment is in-sync with the current Pipefile and lock file.
* As a developer, for tox environments, when I want the updating of the environment to be as fast as possible.  I'm assuming a true "sync" would be faster than creating a new environment because most packages would be the same and there would be less disk IO.
* When deploying and wanting to update the environment on the server.  Same rational as just above, faster because of less disk IO.

Homebrew is more problematic because this happens for every *point* release. With python.org installers (and apt, for this matter) this generally happens only every minor release (e.g. 3.5 -> 3.6) because it tries to maintain binary compatibility between point releases, which makes problems happen less frequently and more obvious to debug. Homebrew also does not try to keep multiple versions of a software around (it does not delete it when you upgrade, but will feel free to clean it up when it sees fit), so you need to take extra care to keep (for example) 3.5 environments working after 3.6 is out. With python.org installers (and apt, again) you can easily have 3.5 and 3.6 installations exist side-by-side.

Note: It is possible achieve the same side-by-side installation in Homebrew, but the procedure is much more awkward, and does not transition well from the default Python installation if you don’t start with the correct setup. This is a deficiency in Homebrew’s design, they acknowledge this, but it is really difficult to do (I don’t blame them to leave things as-is).

If you're using `setuptools < 36.2.0`, then you can put those requirements in `install_requires`:
```diff
 setup.py | 6 +++---
 1 file changed, 3 insertions(+), 3 deletions(-)

diff --git i/setup.py w/setup.py
index fec78409..c929cace 100644
--- i/setup.py
+++ w/setup.py
@@ -279,12 +279,12 @@ setup(
         "idna >= 2.1",
         "asn1crypto >= 0.21.0",
         "six >= 1.4.1",
+        "enum34; python_version < '3'",
+        "ipaddress; python_version < '3'",
+        "cffi >= 1.7; python_implementation != 'PyPy'",
     ],
     tests_require=test_requirements,
     extras_require={
-        ":python_version < '3'": ["enum34", "ipaddress"],
-        ":python_implementation != 'PyPy'": ["cffi >= 1.7"],
-
         "test": test_requirements,
         "docstest": [
             "doc8",
```
Except generated wheels won't be correct, because markers are stripped from the metadata.  `setuptools >= 36.2.0` aims to fix that by automatically moving requirements in `install_requires` that are using markers to `extras_require`, which of course is problematic with this use case...

I think this is talking about "active columns", not "winner columns", which would be a new concept.

I intentionally didn't include a suite of utility methods like this. The TM exposes its fundamental pieces. Callers are welcome to derive the active columns from the active cells, but the TM doesn't do it for them.

For the overall system, it's best for the caller to keep track of the active columns, since they're already passing them into compute. This avoids unnecessary copying and computation.

>One direction where numexpr 3 is going that I won't be able to follow: assignments. I need the functions to be pure expressions because I construct a dependency graph and arrange instructions to avoid recalculating duplicate subexpressions. The use for this is that particle physicists want to fill hundreds of histograms with very similar quantities; manually optimizing them with explicit temporaries conflicts with clarity— they're easier to read as a flat list of expressions. Therefore, I do something similar to numexpr's aggressive mode (but maybe beyond it), reorganizing the expressions to automatically identify temporary quantities*. However, this requires referential transparency: if assignments are allowed, they'd be used as substitutions, and in-place mutations would be completely impossible.
>
>But in the end, that means that I can say I support a subset of numexpr 3's functionality: just the pure expressions.

The first warning is telling you that you're trying to write into pyfolio's cached factors and failing to do so because you don't have appropriate permissions on that file.  Wherever you're seeing that warning, you've got pyfolio installed in the system python, which is probably a bad idea.

The theano warning is indicating that the version of theano you're using was compiled against numpy headers from a version older than the version of numpy you're actually running against.  This isn't **necessarily** broken, but it could be, and the failure mode will be random unexplainable crashes or worse.  The right fix is to uninstall and reinstall theano, compiling against the appropriate numpy.

The matplotlib warnings look like you're calling functions with arguments that it expects to be passed by name rather than position.

Hmm so conv1D ops got deprecated .

The good news is that now we have conv2d on cudnn and you can use it to do conv1d (with several channels).

We'd probably have to code a specific conv1d expression but here's how I do it with conv2d.

Assuming my batch of sentences (of the same length L) are concatenated into a big `M` of shape `(L, D)` (where `D` is the word embedding dimension (the channel dimension if you want), I do

    # Reshape the matrix into a "1D image"
    (l, d), _ = M.dim()
    M = dy.reshape(M, (1, l, d))
    # Do the convolution
    y = dy.conv2d(M, K, [1, 1], is_valid=False)
    # Reshape back into a matrix (if you don't need to do more convolutions)
    z = dy.reshape(y, (L, D))

Where

    K = model.add_parameters((1, kernel_width, D, number_of_kernels))

Are the kernels.

This should also be pretty fast if you have cudnn.

Hope that helps

> I'm not so familiar with unique_pointer, but are there any implications for speed/memory efficiency compared to plain pointers?

Assuming we're talking about Release builds.
My understanding is that there is no noticeable overhead of memory and speed unlike `std::shared_ptr` as long as we don't pass customized deleters to std::unique_ptr's constructor.
According to Scott Meyers's Effective Modern C++:

>"It’s reasonable to assume that, by default, std::unique_ptrs are the same size as raw pointers, and for most operations (including dereferencing), they execute exactly the same instructions. This means you can use them even in situations where memory and cycles are tight. If a raw pointer is small enough and fast enough for you, a std::unique_ptr almost certainly is, too."

BTW, if you have used boost's scoped_ptr before, `std::unique_ptr` is standardized version of it.
MeCab and Moses essentially use very similar smart pointer types.

To explain why we don't need some interfaces like:
`save(vector<ParameterCollection> & models, string)`: there is no case using vector<ParameterCollection>. If they are independent, user can call `save(ParameterCollection, string)` one by one. If they are dependent, they should be only expanded into one `ParameterCollection`.

`save(vector<ParameterCollection> & models, vector<string>)`: user could do it one by one otherwise, they need to create a vector which is the same work calling save one by one.

Similar reason with `load(vector<ParameterCollection> & models, ...)` interfaces.

One thing about this that bothers me *slightly* is that `LookupTable` implements really any function--and the function it implements depends entirely on the table.  In fact the `n_inputs` of the model is dependent on the lookup table that's passed in, and thus can change on a per-instance basis.

This is contrary to how most models are designed, where the inputs are fixed, and tied to the class.  Whether or not that's a good design could be argued, but this might not work well in all contexts that are making this assumption.

The way I see it--since each LookupTable model is really a different model for a different function--that this should instead be a factory function that makes a new `Model` class for a given lookup table.  Here's a quick example I whipped up from your existing `LookupTable` code--it doesn't require much of a change:

```python
class LookupTableBase(Model):
    """
    ...
    """

    linear = False
    fittable = False

    standard_broadcasting = False
    outputs = ('y',)

    lookup_table = abc.abstractproperty()

    _id = 0

    def __init__(self, points=None, method='linear',
                 bounds_error=True, fill_value=np.nan, **kwargs):
        if points is None:
            self._points = tuple(np.arange(x, dtype=np.float)
                                 for x in self.lookup_table.shape)
        else:
            self._points = points
        super(LookupTableBase, self).__init__(**kwargs)

        self.bounds_error = bounds_error
        self.method = method
        self.fill_value = fill_value

    @property
    def points(self):
        return self._points

    def evaluate(self, *inputs):
        inputs = np.array(inputs[: self.n_inputs]).T
        return interpn(self.points, self.lookup_table, inputs)
                       method=self.method, bounds_error=self.bounds_error,
                       fill_value=self.fill_value)


def lookup_table_model(lookup_table, name=None):
    table = np.asanyarray(lookup_table)
    inputs = tuple('x{0}'.format(idx) for idx in range(table.ndim))
    members = {'lookup_table': table, 'inputs': inputs}
    if name is None:
        model_id = LookupTableBase._id
        LookupTableBase._id += 1
        name = 'LookupTable{0}'.format(model_id)

    return type(name, (LookupTableBase,), members)
```

This is just a sketch but that's the basic idea.  It's basically analogous to `@custom_model`, but instead of taking a function as its argument and building a model from that function, it's taking a function given as a lookup table and creating a new model for that function.

This could also be implemented as a `LookupTable.__new__` but I think the more explicit factory function is a bit clearer, especially in analogy with `custom_model`.

I suppose an alternative representation is a list, where each entry corresponds to an output axis, each containing a list of input axes that it depends on.

Therefore, the (x, y, gamma) transform would be: `[(0, 1), (0, 1), (2,)]`

`Polynomial2D(1) & Polynomial2D(1)` -> `[(0, 1), (2, 3)]`

Therefore, if you had a function `dependencies` that calculated this, `is_separable` would just be `[len(x) == 1 for x in model.dependencies()]`.

The "Read Me First" file, as noted in the AFDKO home page, is meant for users who are installing a fully configured AFDKO directory tree. It certainly not should not contain instructions for building the FDK from sources, as most of the AFDKO users already find using command-line tools alarmingly advanced. I did update the "Read Me First" file to indicate that developers should look at the "Build Notes" file. I also updated the latter to note that when building on 64 bit Linux, you need to install the 32-bit support libs. This file actually did already mention using BuildAllLinux.sh, in section 2.1.

I am reluctant to improve the sophistication of the notes, as the AFKO tools have been around for over 30 years, and formatting workflows tend to last only a  few years before something better comes along. When a set of information has some degree of complexity, the value of readability to the developer is certainly worth the maintenance effort, but when it is just a page or two for a small audience, I favor plain text, or at most simple html for the long term.

To @kir-titievsky's point, we need to consider two separate approaches to this:

1. Simple - just remove the "send messages on at a time to the executor" behavior. We get a list of messages on every response from the server. We can just send those to your callback. Kir makes a great point that this is non-deterministic- we have no control over how many items the server sends. Your callback may get anywhere from 1 to 100. 
2. Provide a batch utility. This would look more like the publisher batch - we would have a `max_messages_per_batch` and `max_latency_per_batch` config. The subscriber would receive messages from the server as normal but instead of sending one message at a time, it would hold on to them until *either* threshold is reached. This behavior is clearer but there is one quirk: the subscriber will hold on to overflow messages and give them to the next batch.

WDYT?

btw, the "simple" method is easily possible for *users* to do today with the current interface.

This already exists [here](https://github.com/cloudtools/troposphere/blob/master/troposphere/__init__.py#L407). Note: since the function itself returns a base64 blob, the passed in value should not be encoded.

There are two different validation schemes in troposphere. The first is when a property is added which does simple type checking operations (which is comparing the types or sometimes runs a per-property validator like positive_integer() or network_port()). This should always work with the caveat that AWSHelperFn are always accepted. The second validation step happens in to_dict() where the finalization of the object allows for checking inter-property values. @ikben since that validation step is on an object basis I thought it best to control validation at a finer granularity so you only had to override the validation for the problematic object and get validation for all other objects in the resource. While I could add it at a resource boundary, that is not as natural and would require passing it down through the to_dict() call tree. I'm not opposed to it if there is a need but if the current implementation on an object basis works for the current use case for @tigertoes then I'm inclined to just add it to the object itself. A few considerations that fall out of the above:
1. `GatherSources` currently has no knowledge of tests, which seems desirable - how to decouple?
2. If we find a case where a test package is parallel to a source package and both have non-empty non-ns `__init__.py` we should probably fail-fast.

Perhaps 1 & 2 can be addressed by learning `GatherSources` to care about matching packages across source roots only - regardless of target type owning the package. It could gather all these and implement the sanity check in 2 - either exactly 1 package with non-empty non-ns `__init__`.py or die, and then having verified this, place all code with like packages in the same source pex.

This still falls down though, since the logic would take `proj1/src/python/util/fs.py` and place it in the same pex with `proj2/src/python/util/cache.py` and then `proj2/test/python/util/test_cache.py` would fail to be able to get coverage if `proj1/src/python/util/fs.py` was the ~random winner of the `coverage` mapping ordering. So... more thought needed on rules. This may in fact require knowledge of the test target vs other targets distinction wrt coverage, which implies a custom `GatherSources` task or a new downstream task that takes the `GatherSources` `PythonSources` product and applies test logic to re-jigger pexes into a form useable by `PytestRun`.

ok, it's not really a bug. What happens (and also happened before the PR) is that the maximum output length is dependent on the bucket a sentence falls in, which in turn depends on the other sentences it gets grouped with. This leads to cases where sentences can potentially lead to a different translation with bucketing. This is only really the case when the maximum length was hit with a single sentence. 

I'd say we merge this PR as the behavior stays the same (just now sentences will be grouped differently). We can then separately discuss if we want to change this behavior.

Apart from the backups you do with `backup-push` you are also uploading every WAL (Write-ahead log) file to S3 (that is what the archive_command does). These files contain basically every modification to your database and allow doing point-in-time-recovery of the database.

So my first guess would be that you have a lot of write traffic in the database so you end up uploading a lot of WAL files into S3. What `delete` will do for you is delete WAL files older than any of the full backups left in storage.

But my goal would be to not _require_ people to use `sascfg.py` or `sascfg_personal.py` as a package, imported absolutely, that contains their config info. Instead I would imagine it to be easier if `sascfg.py` is imported, but pointed at a flat config file (.txt, .ini, whatever, not .py). That makes it easy to edit, easy to generate defaults at install time, and enables functionality like
```
sas1 = SASsession(config_file="my_personal_config.txt")
sas2 = SASsession(config_file="my_enterprise_config.txt")
```

I think it's possible to accomplish this while still maintaing backward compatibility with the old method of importing the config as a python module. But to do so cleanly is probably best accomplished with a little refactoring, so I'm just taking a shot at it with IOM.

I totally understand wanting to narrow down problem areas, so I just ran that same code snippet against both the upgraded and non-upgraded versions of music21. My findings were the same regardless of version number. 

`music21.realtime.StreamPlayer(s).play()` produces two separate tones instead of one continuous tone. 

`music21.show()` displays the tie in the GUI editor. 

`music21.show('midi')` (with QuickTime installed) also plays two notes instead of one. It produces the same results as `music21.realtime.StreamPlayer(s).play()`.

My gut suspicion is that something strange is happening at the music21-to-midi conversion step; the notes are converted successfully, but the tie gets lost in translation. After all, converting a half note produces only one tone over two qLs, so it's not like the playback engine is incapable of producing a note with a duration of two qLs. It just happens to play back the two tied quarter notes as two independent quarter notes instead of a single half note. 

Do you have any even vague ideas about what could be causing this? I may have some time to poke around music21's source code on my own after finals season is over, but having a general idea where to investigate first would be helpful in limiting the scope of where to look for problems!

Currently you can do this manually by copying the default unit files from deisctl/units and then adding metadata restrictions under [X-Fleet] (e.g. MachineMetadata=plane=control). You can point the Deis installer to the new unit files by setting the DEISCTL_UNITS environment variable. Of course, you also need to add the appropriate metadata for the CoreOS hosts in your cloud-config (e.g. coreos/fleet/metadata: plane=control). Finally, you need to tell your applications you want to run them on the Data Plane. For this, you need to use deis tags:set (e.g. deis tags:set plane=data).

`OSError: [Errno 5] Input/output error` is something going on with your operating system or file system.  You can use `-vvv` to get a finer-grained picture on where conda is getting tripped up. With all the errors and stack traces in this thread, there are actually multiple things happening I think.  It's not just one bug to fix.  This issue is actually a huge part of the motivation for the prioritized feature work in the 4.3 release.  All of the work dealing with "safety."

For all of the environments and installs that have been hosed, I'm sorry.  It sucks.  It probably hurts me just as much or more than you.

For all of the issues reported here, I'm making sure I at least have a response to it in the code, even if I can't go back and fix something that's already been hosed.  The PR https://github.com/conda/conda/pull/4090 is more work in that direction.

In my case, the underlying system Python is 2.7.5 and I located its `site.py`  and it is not the same as the one created by virtualenv 15.1.0, when executed from a conda Python 3.5 environment.

The `site.py` is base64-encoded inside the `virtualenv.py` file as ``SITE_PY`` variable. I have not tested that the actual file `site.py`  created in the virtual env ``testenv`` really is the one from de-base64-coding that ``SITE_PY`` variable but I trust it is. (The ``site.py``  file in my conda 3.5.3 environment is a different one).

I will happily file a bug at Python upstream if I feel like I understand. I don't have experience with `virtualenv` beyond the one from conda, and I will be mainly offline for some weeks now, so this may not be any time soon (I can't today at any rate).

The conda docs talk quite a bit ([1](https://conda.io/docs/user-guide/getting-started.html#installing-a-package-with-pip), [2](https://conda.io/docs/user-guide/tasks/manage-pkgs.html#installing-non-conda-packages), [3](https://conda.io/docs/user-guide/tasks/manage-environments.html#using-pip-in-an-environment), [4](https://conda.io/docs/user-guide/getting-started.html#managing-packages), [5](https://conda.io/docs/user-guide/configuration/use-condarc.html#add-pip-as-python-dependency-add-pip-as-python-dependency), [6](https://conda.io/docs/user-guide/configuration/use-condarc.html#use-pip-use-pip)) about `pip` and I could find no place where the docs explicitly state that using `pip` and `conda` is a bad idea, so I feel like I'm getting conflicting messages here.
2. I'm guessing the bug I report here is not specific to the tensorflow install case, and could happen again with something else that is pip installed. If you don't believe pip should ever be used with conda then I guess this point will not be convincing.
3. Lastly, the [official "using Anaconda" install instructions](https://www.tensorflow.org/install/install_linux#installing_with_anaconda) for tensorflow still recommend a `pip` install, so I think it's safe to say that a good number of people will install tensorflow via pip even if they are using conda elsewhere. (Maybe it would make sense to suggest an update to those instructions, but I wouldn't be surprised if Google didn't want to suggest an install location from somewhere other than a google owned server for trust reasons.)

@leewhenyoung I have solved this problem.The problem is that I installed Python by Anaconda, then bcolz use libstdc++.so provided by Anaconda, but this libstdc++.so lacks the nessceary symbol used in bcolz.
I solved this problem by use the nm command as follows:
nm /usr/lib/libstdc++.so.6 /usr/lib/libstdc++/so/6.0.22  
Hope it can help you

We can use the predefined encode/decode functions from the `com` package for this as well as the generators of these for custom types. The priority is related to the NSG rule. Rules are executed in priority from high to low and once a rule is satisfied, lower priority rules are not processed. Priority really doesn't have to do with ports--it has to do with conflicting the rules (which is the mechanism through which you can allow or deny ports).

If you run `network nsg show` on the network security group for your VM, it should list all the rules and show the priorities that the portal designated. We do not try to apply a heuristic.

`vm open-port` is a convenience command only and thus it doesn't allow you to specify UDP/TCP for the resulting rule. From the extended help text description, if you want more fine grained control that the `open-port` command allows, you need to use the underlying network commands. In your case it would `az network nsg rule create`.

> Not meaning to offend: this sounds lazy.

We made the change before and decided to go back to how it was before, so it can't really be laziness. It boils down to two competing arguments:

1. Following standard filesystem conventions is a good thing.
2. Having a consistent location across platforms is a good thing.

You (and a steady trickle of other people) clearly think that 1 is more important. The team maintaining it think that 2 is more important. Everyone involved understands both arguments, so discussions about it tend to end up with both sides reiterating their preference.

(There's also *3. changing stuff adds confusion*, which weighs in favour of leaving things as they are)

`Model.__init__` and `Model.validate` do separate validation, However they do affect the internal state:

https://github.com/schematics/schematics/blob/master/schematics/models.py#L234
```python
    def validate(self, partial=False, convert=True, app_data=None, **kwargs):
        """
        Validates the state of the model. If the data is invalid, raises a ``DataError``
        with error messages.
        :param bool partial:
            Allow partial data to validate. Essentially drops the ``required=True``
            settings from field definitions. Default: False
        :param convert:
            Controls whether to perform import conversion before validating.
            Can be turned off to skip an unnecessary conversion step if all values
            are known to have the right datatypes (e.g., when validating immediately
            after the initial import). Default: True
        """
        if not self._data.converted and partial:
            return  # no new input data to validate
        try:
            data = self._convert(validate=True,
                partial=partial, convert=convert, app_data=app_data, **kwargs)
            self._data.valid = data
        except DataError as e:
            valid = dict(self._data.valid)
            valid.update(e.partial_data)
            self._data.valid = valid
            raise
        finally:
            self._data.converted = {}
```

pynsq does reconnect and it does emit log messages about it. What you might be experiencing is the case where pynsq has no good way of knowing that the connection was lost, because there was no FIN or RST packet (this is possible if a firewall/NAT is being annoying or if the remote end abruptly lost connection for example by being unplugged).

There is a "tcp keepalive" feature that can probably be enabled/configured for your OS. nsq does not currently have an application-level keep-alive feature, like ssh and websockets do. (These features use a active method to ensure the remote end of the connection is still responsive.)

I am migrating an existing IoT system that uses a format very similar to Avro + schema-registry.
The device registers a schema and receives unique identifier which it prepends to all outgoing binary data.
My producers then need to do the translation from the old format to the new Avro before appending to the topic, and don't necessarily have the schema cached, so they may need to check the registry.

Basically [this situation](https://gist.github.com/mfe5003/16140f8255f2a9472e1e762d49b9ee15) is what I am looking for, where the schema_id would be known to a client that interacts with the producers.

Brace expression isn't part of the POSIX glob syntax, that is a BASH-ism.

For this example, here's a working version:
``` cwl
class: CommandLineTool
cwlVersion: v1.0
baseCommand:
  - ls
inputs:
  - id: input
    type: string?
outputs:
  - id: output
    type: File[]
    outputBinding:
      glob: 
        - *.png
        - *.txt
requirements:
  - class: DockerRequirement
    dockerPull: 'ubuntu:16.04'
  - class: InitialWorkDirRequirement
    listing:
      - entryname: a.txt
        entry: a
      - entryname: b.png
        entry: b
stdout: c.txt
```
Or with an inline YAML list: `glob: [ *.png, *. txt ]`

Hi Peter,

Thanks for taking a look! I have some questions about your requests.

a) Certainly, this is something we had talked about doing.

b) Reading the code, `tmp_outdir_prefix` is ignored if `outdir` is supplied, so won't this cause cwltool to bypass the cache if `outdir` is supplied? Even using the `outdir` for the job (rather than the `outdir` for the whole workflow) would fail as it is different every time the workflow is run, and for the cache to work we must be able to find it between runs.

Something else I'm missing here?

c,d) You are suggesting that our output files are cached by virtue of the cache directory being `tmp_outdir_prefix`, so that all we need to write beyond that is a JSON output object - which output object you are referring to? The one returned by `collect_outputs` is passed along independent of whether the cache is used or not, so I am assuming you mean some other output object... or I may be missing the significance of the one returned by `collect_outputs` beyond being a map of paths.

Either way, for the reasons above (b) I don't see how we can reliably use `tmp_outdir_prefix` when it is overridden by `outdir`. Any help appreciated there!

In the current implementation I am just copying whatever appears in the `outdir` for the job into the cache. Since each job has its own `outdir` which is distinct from the overall `outdir` for the workflow, whatever appears in there is guaranteed to be produced by the job in question, correct?

This is the least invasive approach I was able to come up with, in particular preserving the call to `collect_outputs` so that the system does not care whether the outputs are a result of the job running or simply copied from the cache. Since the result of each job is simply its outputs, this is the narrowest scope the cache could operate and the least impact on the existing system.

The `cachedir` can be anywhere as long as it is persistent between jobs and accessible after the workflow is complete.

Open to all perspectives otherwise of course! Thanks for taking the time to work through it.

The first thing I would look for if your variables aren't being set is the alias for awsume.

Awsume's shell script must be sourced when executed so that the environment variables will be set to the current shell, instead of creating a subshell, setting the subshell's environment variables and closing the subshell.

Awsume's installation should have put an alias definition in your `.bashrc`, `.bash_profile`, etc. for you that looks like this:

```
alias awsume=". awsume"
```
Some users didn't have one of those files on their system when they installed awsume, in which case you could create the file and reinstall awsume. If that exists in one of your login files, then check to make sure the alias exists by running `alias` to list your current shell's aliases. Also note that once you install awsume, you may need to open a new terminal for the alias to be set by the login file.

If everything looks good, but is still not working please let us know!

There is no notion of providing several options inside the configuration. The code you posted will simply assign the list `[2, 3, 4}` to x. 

For trying multiple options I often use a separate script that imports the experiment and runs it many times. So e.g. like this:
``` python
from basic import ex
x_choices = [2, 3, 4]
for x in x_choices:
    ex.run(config_updates={'x': x})
```
A grid search can then also easily be implemented using `itertools.product`.

If you are interested in parallel execution, then I agree with @trickmeyer: the simplest way is to use the commanline interface, and just call the experiment many times with different parameters.

A different strategy, that is only partially supported at this point is to use the queueing function of sacred (`-q`) to store the configurations that you plan on running in the database with the status `QUEUED`, and have a set of workers that periodically query the database for such entries and run them. The querying is fully supported, but there is currently no code for the workers. I have a prototype locally, that should be included in sacred soon. But I can also post it here if you are interested.

@wintersteiger 
> I see your reasoning, but at the time of running mk_make.py we don't really know whether DESTDIR will be used later or not, so that branch would have to be in the Makefile. If we detect an empty DESTDIR during mk_make.py then we would install into /Library/... even if DESTDIR is non-empty at the time of make install, right?

No, I'm proposing something far more simple. Basically when generating the install command in the makefile rule for the Python bindings on OSX we will detect if the python package directory is in the prefix.

If it is then we emit the normal that we do normally.

```
	@cp z3*.py $(DESTDIR)$(PREFIX)/lib/python3.5/site-packages

```

if the python package directory is not in the prefix (and we are on OSX) then we emit this

```
        @echo WARNING python bindings are being installed outside of $(PREFIX)
	@cp z3*.py $(DESTDIR)/Library/Python/3.5/site-packages/ 
```

If we are not on OSX emission of the above is not allowed and running the python script will fail with an error.

If we do it this way

* We are still respecting ``DESTDIR`` in all cases
* If DESTDIR is empty (which it normally is) the bindings will still be installed on OSX to the right location for them to work.
* We emit a warning at install time about the dodgy install that we are doing. We could (and should) emit a warning at configure time too.
* On other platforms we can still ban installing outside of ``PREFIX``.

Does that make sense?

For any filesystem, the walk property is intended to be a shortcut, but when you want to have a custom walker, you should:

* declare a `CustomWalker` inheriting from `fs.walk.Walker`
* instantiate a `BoundWalker` instance that uses your `CustomWalker`
* use your `BoundWalker` as you would use a walker (or `fs.walk`)


For instance:
1. create a `NoHiddenWalker` that scans directories only if they are not hidden (in Unix-style):
  ```python
   import fs.walk
   class NoHiddenWalker(fs.walk.Walker):
      def check_scan_dir(self, fs, path, info):
         return not info.name.startswith('.')
   ```
2. Open your filesystem and obtain a `BoundWalker`:
   ```python
   my_fs = fs.open_fs("~")   
   my_walker = Boundwalker(my_fs, walker_class=NoHiddenWalker)
   ```
3. Use your walker normally:
   ```python
   for x in my_walker.files():
      # ... #
   ```

From https://tools.ietf.org/html/rfc6238#section-6:
```
   Because of possible clock drifts between a client and a validation
   server, we RECOMMEND that the validator be set with a specific limit
   to the number of time steps a prover can be "out of synch" before
   being rejected.

   This limit can be set both forward and backward from the calculated
   time step on receipt of the OTP value.  If the time step is
   30 seconds as recommended, and the validator is set to only accept
   two time steps backward, then the maximum elapsed time drift would be
   around 89 seconds, i.e., 29 seconds in the calculated time step and
   60 seconds for two backward time steps.

   This would mean the validator could perform a validation against the
   current time and then two further validations for each backward step
   (for a total of 3 validations).  Upon successful validation, the
   validation server can record the detected clock drift for the token
   in terms of the number of time steps.  When a new OTP is received
   after this step, the validator can validate the OTP with the current
   timestamp adjusted with the recorded number of time-step clock drifts
   for the token.

   Also, it is important to note that the longer a prover has not sent
   an OTP to a validation system, the longer (potentially) the
   accumulated clock drift between the prover and the verifier.  In such
   cases, the automatic resynchronization described above may not work
   if the drift exceeds the allowed threshold.  Additional
   authentication measures should be used to safely authenticate the
   prover and explicitly resynchronize the clock drift between the
   prover and the validator.
```
It seems unambiguous that a "max_skew" parameter is needed, in number of time steps. The questions I have are:

* Is there any need to parameterize the forward and backward max skew separately?
* How exactly should resync work? i.e. if I record that the prover sent a proof from 3 timesteps ago, do I immediately adjust the expected skew to -3 and recenter the window of acceptance around it, or what?
* How to incorporate resync into the pyotp API, if at all?

I think setting an environment variable is the most consistent way across libraries, and as it's something that we don't want people committing to their codebase, it has an appropriate level of "impermanence".

While we're here, if you can set an environment variable called `PACT_INTERACTION_RERUN_COMMAND`, which has the python command template to run a verification, the ruby pact library will spit out a list of "rerun commands", one for each failure, that will tell the user exactly what to run to execute a single interaction.

eg. the command to run a single verification in ruby is `bundle exec rake pact:verify:at[<PACT_URI>] PACT_DESCRIPTION=\"<PACT_DESCRIPTION>\" PACT_PROVIDER_STATE=\"<PACT_PROVIDER_STATE>\"`

The ruby code substitutes in the uri, description and state to create the command to run. 

This is already implemented in the ruby codebase, so it might make more sense if you just try it and see.

Is there any use case for separately resetting the database and nuking
`/var/lib/pulp/{content,published}/*`, either right now or in the immediate
future? I can't think of any. If the goal is to reset Pulp, one will almost
certainly want to do these two actions together. Doing just one action will
probably put Pulp in a bad state. How about combining the `reset_database` and
`clean_filesystem` methods into a single `reset_pulp` method?

Is there any benefit to providing this functionality as a mixin? At this point,
I don't see any. Resetting Pulp doesn't require access to any instance or class
attributes, and currently, we explicitly call the `reset_database` and
`clean_filesystem` methods. On the other hand, inheritance bears significant
legibility costs. Developers reading the code at hand must traverse the entire
inheritance tree for any given class to understand how it works. Mixins also
fill the namespaces of the child classes, making accidental method overrides
more likely.

How about providing a single `pulp_smash.utils.reset_pulp` function?

```python
def reset_pulp(server_config):
    """Reset Pulp's database, nuke accumulated files, and restart services."""
    services = tuple((
        cli.Service(server_config, service) for service in
        {'httpd', 'pulp_celerybeat', 'pulp_resource_manager', 'pulp_workers'}

The `r` is not required.

```python
r'my step (\d+)' == 'my step (\d+)'

It is there so that Python does not try to parse `\d` as an [escape sequence](https://docs.python.org/3/reference/lexical_analysis.html) (search for "Escape Sequence" on that page) and output a warning:

```shell
$ python -W all -c 'print("\d")'
<string>:1: DeprecationWarning: invalid escape sequence \d
\d
```

Hi Mathias,

I'm not aware of anything in particular within ``cmd2`` which would limit the performance of what you are trying to do, at least if you are dealing with typical human reaction-time type timescales.

Do you have any example code you can share that demonstrates these performance problems?

Whenever I encounter a performance problem, the first thing I do is profile the application to get actual hard data so I can understand what is slow.  You can use Python's build-in [cProfile](https://docs.python.org/3/library/profile.html) module to do this.

Once I find the actual problem, if it isn't something trivial to fix then I typically use [Cython](http://cython.org) to optimize the actual bottleneck.

If you are dealing with a very simple command/argument parsing situation and you don't mind giving up some ``cmd2`` features like command history, output redirection, piping command output to shell commands, hooks, command timing, etc., then you can bypass a bunch of ``cmd2`` logic and call the ``onecmd()`` method instead of the ``onecmd_plus_hooks()`` or ``runcmds_plus_hooks()`` methods.

The slowest part of cmd2 is the **pyparsing** routines which parse everything the user types in.  The parsing logic is quite complicated due to all of the features supported and possible edge cases.  It has never been a problem when dealing with humans typing commands in on a keyboard.  If you are trying to work at machine speeds, then this could present a bottleneck.  In a perfect world, I would like to replace the use of **pyparsing** in ``cmd2`` with something faster, but it would be a lot of work and I don't have the time for that anytime soon.

I hope some of that is helpful ;-)

Good luck!

-Todd

So, maybe this is a bit out there, but what about just not running an ACME challenge on `*.domain.tld` to begin with? Consider these scenarios (for DNS challenge only):

- User submits for `sub1.domain.tld` and `sub2.domain.tld`: DNS challenge is required for both since they are not related to root domain.
- User submits for `domain.tld` and `sub1.domain.tld`: DNS challenge is required for both because legally they may have sold a subdomain
- User submits for `domain.tld` and `*.domain.tld`: A DNS challenge is required just for `domain.tld` since this dictates ownership and plan to assign to any subdomain

From a security standpoint, it really makes checking anything except the root-most level submitted pointless if using a DNS challenge. Because if I have a wildcard for `domain.tld`, I can do anything below that. Or if I have a wildcard for `sub1.domain.tld`, I can do anything below that, too. The fact that it's two records on one TXT further demonstrates that. The second scenario here is really just a "sure why not."

not same domain, but subdomain of it.
For example if there is a domain webczatnet.pl (my own domain) that is a zone boundary with a SOA record, and you have a domain for example bin.webczatnet.pl with record
bin.webczatnet.pl IN CNAME webczatnet.pl.
And then you do a lookup like the dns-rfc2136 plugin does to find the soa
bin.webczatnet.pl IN SOA
Then you don't get an empty response as for no soa record for bin.webczatnet.pl, you get redirected. The answer contains a cname record pointing to webczatnet.pl, then the soa record of webczatnet.pl. That violates the zone root finding logic of the plugin. It tries to find a root of the zone by locating it's soa, but it assumes it found the zone root when it gets a non empty answer section. but in this case it will be non empty even though it is not a zone root. it should then either go up (that is what this plugin does), or replace the domain of the lookup with the owner of the SOA record returned.

I bet this also applies to nginx...

Option 1, tell the user to run:

```
for server in dovecot,exim,nginx ; do
  certbot -a nginx -i $server
done
```
Advantages: doesn't require code changes. Disadvantages: doesn't work with renewal unless the user does something like put another invocation of `certbot install` in `--renew-hook` (which means it probably blocks on fixing the `install` verb).

Option 2: 

```
certbot -a nginx -i dovecot,exim,nginx
```

Disadvantage: requires code changes, may need a new variable in renewal conf files in order to turn `installer` into a list without creating backward incompatibility. Advantage: simpler UI, allows us to compute the union of domains found by all of the installers for the `run` verb.

This happens because, as you're using a private blockchain, there's no discovery for it in the main (as in "the one the clients will look for the discovery room) server (`transport01`), and you're on another server (`transport03`).
As the matrix client tries to create it, it fails, because the room can't be created in a server different than the one you're on now.

You have two options for running matrix transport with a private blockchain. Either:
- At least once, run the client specifying the transport01 as the server, with `--matrix-server="http://transport01.raiden.network"`. This will create the room there, and then you can go back to your server, and it'll gladly join this room, as expected (or stay in the 01 server, if you want)
- Specify a different "main" server (changing [this default config](https://github.com/raiden-network/raiden/blob/e7c82a12fcb2db764a47be514c8f3c6dee0f4ecc/raiden/app.py#L76), which isn't exposed through config options yet). You need to do this for all the clients you want connected to this private blockchain, as they need to join the same "discovery" room to be able to find each other.

The Channel groups all parameters that relate to a specific channel. From QCoDeS 0.1.5 we have added that concept so that you can group the parameters in a logical way and do instrument.channels.ch1.parameter rather than instrument.ch1_parameter and so on. This makes it much easier to write code that can do the same operations for multiple channels such as an oscilloscope that typically has at least 2 identical channels. 

The ScopeArray is an array parameter. This needs special treatment because the scope returns an array of data representing the time series. The time series has a variable length, offset and time resolution depending on the settings of the scope. In this particular scope you need to get the preamble to find the data to calculate the x-axis array

Yea the CLI was a pain >< especially when I did my multi-segment with seek fork, I kept having to remember a bunch of arguments that were required (--http... something something) or it just behave like the original. Interactive mode would be good. Probably want to fix all the broken stuff in the current version first though, so we have a stable version to work from as a base. This would probably be version 1.0.0. I vote for using [semver](http://semver.org/) versioning syntax.

On the note of backwards compatibility, I think we shouldn't worry about it too much. The way I'd like to see Streamlink go is to become more like a proxy server than it is now. i.e. drop other output transport methods (to the player), like pipes, and just output using the input stream transport method (i.e HTTP, RTMP). Then Streamlink's job would be to act as an accelerating proxy (using multiple segments and buffering ahead and behind). The player end point would be expected to understand the streaming method (i.e. HLS, HTTP Progressive, HDS, RTMP). This is useful because often the transport mechanism contains useful metadata for the player, such as information about timing required for seek.

```
walltime=60, cache=False, sites='all'
```
How did swift keep options like that separate (sorry for not digging into the code there yet)? The only way that occurs to me is to have the entire specification of the work to be done separate (as is done in Lobster, CRAB, makeflow, and every other workflow system I have used), but I think one of the big advantages of Parsl is that, using decorators, you can **label** the work itself rather than **map** the work to a separate specification describing the work. It seems to me that if we want to eliminate any config info in the decorator,  we need to either 1. introduce a conventional, external specification of the work or 2. seriously restrict functionality such that there are no per-app configuration options. I don't like either of those choices, but maybe there are some good alternatives that are not occurring to me?

To clarify the meaning of the scores:
`deepsea_effect` is the score that Zhou et al. 2015 used to calculate the e-value - it is therefore not what the website displays as `effect (Log2)` and it is also not the e-value itself. How to use this score:
1) Either use the precomputed values from the DeepSEA website or calculate `deepsea_effect` of 1M randomly selected variants
2) Calculate the `deepsea_effect` of the new variants
3) Calculate the e-value

To get the values that are displayed on the website as `effect (Log2)` the `logit` score of variant effect prediction can be used - since `logit` uses the natural logarithm for calculation the values produced there have to be devided by the constant `np.log(2)` to get the same numerical values that are displayed on the deepsea website.
